# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

This section contains **board-exam-style questions** modeled on actual KTU question paper patterns, with complete model answers. Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference.

---

## 📌 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define the fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane. List any two functions of membrane proteins.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> The **fluid mosaic model**, proposed by **S. J. Singer and G. L. Nicolson in 1972**, describes the plasma membrane as a **thin (~7–10 nm) phospholipid bilayer in which proteins float like icebergs in a sea of lipid**. The lipid molecules are arranged with their **hydrophilic phosphate heads** facing the aqueous exterior and interior, and their **hydrophobic fatty acid tails** facing each other. The membrane is **fluid** because lipids and proteins can move laterally, and **mosaic** because proteins are dispersed unevenly throughout.

**Two functions of membrane proteins:**
1. **Transport** — channel and carrier proteins mediate the movement of ions and polar molecules across the lipid bilayer.
2. **Signal transduction** — receptor proteins bind signaling molecules (hormones, neurotransmitters) and trigger intracellular responses.

*(Any other valid function — enzymatic activity, cell adhesion, cell recognition — accepted for 1 mark each.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) and Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum (SER).**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Feature | Rough ER | Smooth ER |
|---|---|---|
| **Ribosomes** | Present (on cytoplasmic face) | Absent |
| **Appearance** | Studded, "rough" | Tubular, "smooth" |
| **Main functions** | Synthesis of secretory/membrane proteins, N-linked glycosylation, protein folding | Lipid/steroid synthesis, detoxification (Cytochrome P450), Ca²⁺ storage (e.g., sarcoplasmic reticulum) |
| **Abundance** | High in protein-secreting cells (e.g., plasma cells, pancreatic acinar cells) | High in steroid-secreting cells (e.g., Leydig cells, adrenal cortex) and hepatocytes |

---

## 📌 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each module offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**, each carrying **14 marks** with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

#### **Q.A.(a) Describe the ultrastructure and functions of mitochondria. Add a note on the endosymbiotic theory.** (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Ultrastructure (3 marks):**
Mitochondria are **double-membrane-bound, rod-shaped organelles** (~0.5–1 µm in diameter, 1–10 µm in length). Components include:
- **Outer membrane** — smooth, contains **porin proteins (VDAC)** allowing passage of molecules ≤ 5 kDa.
- **Intermembrane space** — contains protons (H⁺) pumped by the ETC, generating the **proton-motive force**.
- **Inner membrane** — highly folded into **cristae** (increases surface area); contains the **electron transport chain (Complexes I–IV)**, **ATP synthase (Complex V)**, and **cardiolipin** (a unique lipid making it impermeable).
- **Matrix** — gel-like interior containing **mtDNA** (circular, ~16.6 kb, 37 genes), **70S ribosomes**, **TCA cycle enzymes**, and **mitochondrial granules** (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ storage).

**(ii) Functions (3 marks):**
1. **ATP production** via oxidative phosphorylation (~32 ATP/glucose) — the cell's primary energy currency.
2. **TCA (Krebs) cycle** — completes glucose oxidation in the matrix.
3. **β-oxidation of fatty acids** — produces acetyl-CoA, NADH, FADH₂.
4. **Calcium homeostasis** — mitochondrial Ca²⁺ uptake regulates cytosolic Ca²⁺ signaling.
5. **Apoptosis regulation** — releases **cytochrome c** → activates caspase cascade.
6. **Heat production** — via **UCP1 (thermogenin)** in brown adipose tissue (non-shivering thermogenesis).

**(iii) Endosymbiotic Theory (1 mark):**
Proposed by **Lynn Margulis (1970)**, this theory states that mitochondria evolved from an **aerobic α-proteobacterium** engulfed by a primitive eukaryotic cell ~1.5 billion years ago. **Evidence includes:** (a) double membrane (derived from host phagocytic vesicle + bacterial outer membrane), (b) **circular, naked mtDNA** without histones, (c) **70S ribosomes** resembling bacterial ribosomes, (d) **binary fission** replication independent of cell cycle, (e) **maternal inheritance** pattern.

---

#### **Q.A.(b) Explain the structure and functions of the Nucleus. Briefly describe the nucleolus.** (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Nuclear Structure (4 marks):**
- **Nuclear envelope**: double membrane perforated by **nuclear pore complexes (NPCs)** (composed of **nucleoporins**, ~125 MDa). NPCs allow selective transport (mRNA out, proteins with NLS in).
- **Perinuclear space** (~20–40 nm) between the two membranes.
- **Outer membrane** is continuous with the **Rough ER**.
- **Nuclear lamina**: meshwork of **intermediate filaments (lamins A, B, C)** underlying the inner membrane; provides mechanical support; anchors chromatin.
- **Chromatin**: DNA + histone proteins (H2A, H2B, H3, H4); exists as:
  - **Euchromatin** — less condensed, transcriptionally **active**.
  - **Heterochromatin** — densely packed, transcriptionally **silent** (e.g., Barr body = inactivated X chromosome).
- **Nucleoplasm** — gel-like ground substance.

**Functions of nucleus:**
1. Stores **genetic information (DNA)**.
2. Controls **gene expression** (transcription).
3. Site of **DNA replication** (S phase).
4. Houses **nucleolus** for ribosome biogenesis.
5. Controls cellular differentiation and metabolism.

**(ii) Nucleolus (3 marks):**
- **Non-membrane-bound**, dense, spherical structure within the nucleus.
- Composed of **fibrillar centers**, **dense fibrillar component**, and **granular component**.
- **Functions:**
  1. **Transcription of rRNA genes** (by RNA Polymerase I) — produces 45S pre-rRNA.
  2. **Processing of pre-rRNA** → 18S, 5.8S, 28S rRNAs.
  3. **Assembly of ribosomal subunits** (40S from 18S rRNA + proteins; 60S from 28S + 5.8S + 5S rRNA + proteins).
  4. Subunits exported through NPCs to cytoplasm, where they assemble into functional 80S ribosomes.
- **Biomedical note:** Nucleolar hypertrophy is a hallmark of cancer cells (used in histopathology — **AgNOR staining**).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

#### **Q.B.(a) With a neat diagram, describe the fluid mosaic model of the plasma membrane. List the various transport mechanisms across the membrane.** (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Fluid Mosaic Model (4 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram.)*
- **Phospholipid bilayer**: Two layers of phospholipids with **hydrophilic heads** (phosphate + glycerol) facing outward and **hydrophobic tails** (fatty acids) facing inward. This creates a **selectively permeable barrier**.
- **Cholesterol molecules** interspersed in the bilayer — prevent tight packing at low temperatures (maintain fluidity) and restrict movement at high temperatures (stabilize).
- **Integral (transmembrane) proteins** — span the bilayer; include channels, carriers, receptors, and pumps. Move laterally within the membrane.
- **Peripheral proteins** — loosely attached to membrane surface, often linked to cytoskeleton.
- **Glycoproteins and glycolipids** — carbohydrate chains projecting outward, forming the **glycocalyx** (cell recognition, blood group antigens, MHC).
- **Fluidity** is maintained by: (a) unsaturated fatty acids (kinks prevent tight packing), (b) cholesterol, (c) lateral diffusion of lipids and proteins.
- **Evidence for fluidity:** Mouse-human cell fusion experiments (Frye & Edidin, 1970) showed mixing of membrane proteins.

**(ii) Transport Mechanisms (3 marks):**

| Type | Energy | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple diffusion** | None | O₂, CO₂, small nonpolar molecules |
| **Facilitated diffusion** | None (uses carrier/channel) | Glucose (GLUT), ions through channels |
| **Osmosis** | None (water movement) | Water via aquaporins |
| **Active transport** | ATP | Na⁺/K⁺ pump, Ca²⁺ pump |
| **Vesicular transport** | ATP | Endocytosis, exocytosis |

---

#### **Q.B.(b) Explain the structure and functions of Lysosomes. Add a note on lysosomal storage diseases.** (7 marks)

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Structure (3 marks):**
- **Membrane-bound spherical vesicles** (~0.1–1.2 µm diameter) derived from the **trans-Golgi network** (via mannose-6-phosphate receptor pathway).
- Bounded by a **single phospholipid membrane** that is highly **glycosylated** on the inner face (protects membrane from autodigestion).
- Contains **~50 acid hydrolases**, including:
  - **Proteases** (cathepsins)
  - **Lipases**
  - **Nucleases**
  - **Glycosidases**
  - **Phosphatases**
- Internal pH maintained at **~4.5–5.0** by a **V-type H⁺-ATPase pump** that actively transports H⁺ into the lumen.
- Enzymes are synthesized in the RER, glycosylated, tagged with **mannose-6-phosphate (M6P)** in the cis-Golgi, and sorted to lysosomes via **M6P receptors**.

**(ii) Functions (2 marks):**
1. **Intracellular digestion** — phagosomes fuse with lysosomes to form **phagolysosomes** (e.g., macrophage killing of bacteria).
2. **Autophagy** — engulfment of damaged organelles (e.g., mitochondria) into **autophagosomes**, which fuse with lysosomes for recycling.
3. **Apoptosis** — controlled release of cathepsins activates the caspase cascade.
4. **Extracellular digestion** — e.g., osteoclasts use lysosomal enzymes to resorb bone matrix.
5. **Defense** — neutrophils deploy lysosomal contents (e.g., defensins) to kill pathogens.

**(iii) Lysosomal Storage Diseases (2 marks):**
These are inherited disorders caused by deficiency of specific lysosomal enzymes, leading to substrate accumulation within lysosomes:

| Disease | Deficient Enzyme | Accumulating Substrate |
|---|---|---|
| **Tay-Sachs** | Hexosaminidase A | GM2 ganglioside (in neurons) |
| **Pompe disease** | Acid α-glucosidase | Glycogen (in muscle) |
| **Gaucher disease** | Glucocerebrosidase | Glucocerebroside (in macrophages) |
| **Niemann-Pick** | Sphingomyelinase | Sphingomyelin |
| **Fabry disease** | α-galactosidase A | Globotriaosylceramide |
| **Krabbe disease** | Galactocerebrosidase | Galactocerebroside |

**Clinical features** include hepatosplenomegaly, neurological deterioration, and skeletal abnormalities. **Treatment** includes enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) and substrate reduction therapy (SRT).

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cell Structure Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Mitochondrial ATP yield** — Many students write 36 or 38 ATP. The *current accepted* value is **30–32 ATP**. Writing 36 ATP (old textbook value) may cost you 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase stoichiometry** — It is **3 Na⁺ OUT, 2 K⁺ IN, 1 ATP hydrolyzed**. Reversing this (2 Na⁺ out, 3 K⁺ in) is the #1 most common error — *deducted 1 mark by every examiner*.
> 
> 3. **Ribosome size** — Eukaryotic ribosomes = **80S** (60S + 40S). Prokaryotic = **70S** (50S + 30S). Don't confuse the two.
> 
> 4. **Fluid Mosaic Model author** — Write **Singer & Nicolson, 1972** (not just 1970 or "Robertson"). Wrong year = partial mark loss.
> 
> 5. **Nernst equation sign** — Log ratio is **OUTSIDE / INSIDE** for cations (e.g., $E_K = 61.5 \log(5/140) = -89$ mV). For anions, it is **INSIDE / OUTSIDE** ($E_{Cl} = -61.5 \log(4/103) = -85$ mV). Reversing this gives wrong signs.
> 
> 6. **Lysosome pH** — Write **4.5–5.0** (acidic), maintained by **H⁺-ATPase**. Many students write pH 7, which is cytoplasmic — wrong.
> 
> 7. **Centriole arrangement** — **9+0 triplet arrangement** (NOT 9+2, which is for motile cilia/flagella). Examiners catch this distinction.
> 
> 8. **"Smooth ER stores Ca²⁺"** — Specifically true for **sarcoplasmic reticulum** in muscle cells; specify this for full marks.
> 
> 9. **Diagram requirement** — Even if a question doesn't explicitly ask for a diagram, a *neat labeled diagram* fetches 1–2 *grace marks*. Always include one in 7-mark answers.
> 
> 10. **Avoid colloquial language** — Use precise terms: "phospholipid bilayer" not "fat layer"; "nuclear pore complex" not "hole in nucleus"; "oxidative phosphorylation" not "energy making".

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🎯 High-Yield Bullet Summary (Print-and-Revise)

- **Cell theory** = Schleiden (plants) + Schwann (animals) + Virchow (Omnis cellula e cellula). Three tenets: structural/functional unit, all organisms from cells, cells from pre-existing cells.
- **Prokaryotes** = no nucleus, circular DNA, 70S ribosomes. **Eukaryotes** = true nucleus, linear DNA, 80S ribosomes, membrane-bound organelles.
- **Plasma membrane** = 7–10 nm phospholipid bilayer (fluid mosaic model, Singer & Nicolson 1972). Composed of ~50% lipids, ~40% proteins, ~10% cholesterol, ~2% carbohydrates. **Glycocalyx** = carbohydrate coat for cell recognition.
- **Membrane transport** = 4 passive types (simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, filtration) + active (primary ATP-driven, secondary coupled) + bulk (endo/exocytosis).
- **Nucleus** = largest organelle, double membrane with NPCs, contains chromatin (eu- + hetero-) and nucleolus. Houses DNA, controls gene expression, site of rRNA synthesis.
- **Nucleolus** = non-membrane, site of rRNA transcription (RNA Pol I) and ribosomal subunit assembly. **Cancer cells** have prominent/prominent nucleoli (AgNOR).
- **Mitochondria** = double-membrane, has cristae (inner folds), matrix (mtDNA, 70S ribosomes, TCA enzymes), intermembrane space. **~32 ATP/glucose** via oxidative phosphorylation. Endosymbiotic origin (Margulis, 1970). Contains cardiolipin. Releases cytochrome c in apoptosis.
- **RER** = ribosome-studded → protein synthesis, folding (chaperones), N-glycosylation. Abundant in secretory cells.
- **SER** = no ribosomes → lipid/steroid synthesis, detoxification (CYP450), Ca²⁺ storage (sarcoplasmic reticulum).
- **Golgi** = cis → medial → trans → TGN. Site of post-translational modification, sorting, packaging. Produces secretory vesicles, lysosomes, membrane proteins.
- **Lysosomes** = membrane-bound, ~50 acid hydrolases, pH 4.5–5.0. Functions: phagocytosis, autophagy, apoptosis. Storage diseases: Tay-Sachs, Pompe, Gaucher, Niemann-Pick.
- **Peroxisomes** = catalase, oxidases. β-oxidation of VLCFAs, H₂O₂ detox. Zellweger syndrome = peroxisome biogenesis defect.
- **Ribosomes** = 80S (60S + 40S) in eukaryotes. Free ribosomes → cytosolic proteins; membrane-bound → secretory/membrane proteins.
- **Cytoskeleton** = microfilaments (actin, 7 nm), intermediate filaments (10 nm, vimentin/keratin/lamin/neurofilament), microtubules (tubulin, 25 nm). Functions: shape, motility, transport, division.
- **Centrosome** = pair of centrioles with **9+0 triplet microtubule arrangement**. Organizes mitotic spindle. (Motile cilia/flagella have 9+2 doublet + 2 central.)
- **Fick's Law** = $J = -D \cdot dC/dx$. Used in dialysis, drug delivery, oxygen diffusion.
- **Nernst equation** = $E = (RT/zF) \ln([out]/[in])$. At 37°C, $RT/F \ln = 61.5$ mV. Calculate E_K ≈ −89 mV, E_Na ≈ +63 mV.
- **van't Hoff** = $\Pi = iMRT$. 0.9% NaCl is isotonic (≈ 308 mOsm/L).

### 🔑 Key Mnemonics

- **Organelles of Energy:** *"**M**itochondria make **A**TP"* (adenosine triphosphate).
- **Phospholipid orientation:** *"Heads OUT, Tails IN"* — hydrophilic heads face aqueous environments; hydrophobic tails hide inside.
- **NADH vs FADH₂ ATP yield:** *"NADH is Naughty — gives 2.5; FADH₂ is Friendly but Fewer — gives 1.5."*
- **Na⁺/K⁺ pump:** *"Pump 3 Na OUT, bring 2 K IN"* (never reverse this!).
- **Cytoskeleton diameters (small → large):** *"**M**icrofilament is **M**inute (~7), **I**ntermediate is **I**n-between (~10), **M**icrotubule is **M**assive (~25)."*
- **Endosymbiotic evidence:** *"**D**ouble membrane, **C**ircular DNA, **R**ibosomes like bacteria, **B**inary fission"* → DC-RB.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For this topic, KTU examiners love asking the **comparison table** (prokaryote vs eukaryote, RER vs SER, mitochondria vs chloroplast, lysosomes vs peroxisomes). Practice drawing **neat, labeled diagrams** with a pen on paper — not just on screen — because your diagram quality directly affects 2–3 marks in 7-mark answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organelles & Their Functions. Cell Membrane - Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT205 - ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING (BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Organelles & Their Functions. Cell Membrane - Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cell — Foundation of Life

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **cell** is the fundamental structural and functional unit of all living organisms, capable of independent existence and reproduction. Each cell contains specialized internal compartments called **organelles** ("little organs") that perform specific biochemical functions necessary for cellular survival, homeostasis, and reproduction.

A typical human **eukaryotic cell** measures between **10–100 micrometers (µm)** in diameter and is bounded by a plasma membrane enclosing a gel-like cytoplasm and a nucleus housing deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cell as a Factory

Imagine a **modern automated factory** working 24/7:

| Cell Organelle | Factory Analogy | Real Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Nucleus** | Factory Manager's Office | Stores DNA, controls all activities |
| **Mitochondria** | Power Plant / Generators | Produces ATP (energy currency) |
| **Ribosomes** | Assembly Line Workers | Synthesize proteins |
| **ER (Rough)** | Production Floor with machines | Folds & transports proteins |
| **ER (Smooth)** | Chemical Mixing Section | Synthesizes lipids, detoxifies |
| **Golgi Apparatus** | Packaging & Shipping Center | Modifies, sorts, packages proteins |
| **Lysosomes** | Waste Disposal & Recycling Unit | Breaks down waste, dead organelles |
| **Peroxisomes** | Chemical Treatment Plant | Neutralizes toxins, breaks fatty acids |
| **Cytoskeleton** | Steel Framework & Conveyor Belts | Provides shape, mechanical support |
| **Centrosome** | Project Planning Department | Organizes cell division |
| **Cell Membrane** | Factory Security Gate & Walls | Controls entry/exit, communication |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to **list, locate, and functionally correlate** each organelle with its physiological role. Memory diagrams (labelled cell structures) are recurring short-answer questions in the KTU board exams.

---

## 1.2 Organelles — Quick-Reference Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Organelle:**
> An organelle is a membrane-bound (or non-membrane in case of ribosomes) subcellular structure within a eukaryotic cell that carries out a discrete, specialized function.

### 1.2.1 The Nucleus (Control Center)

The nucleus is the **largest organelle**, typically **5–10 µm** in diameter, and contains the cell's genetic blueprint (DNA). It is enclosed by a **double-membrane nuclear envelope** perforated by **nuclear pores** (≈ **9 nm** diameter). The dense region inside is the **nucleolus**, where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized.

- **Functions:** Storage of genetic information, DNA replication, transcription (mRNA synthesis), ribosome assembly.
- **Analogy:** The "library + CEO's office" — stores master blueprints and issues orders.

### 1.2.2 Mitochondria (Powerhouse)

Rod-shaped, **0.5–1.0 µm** wide and **1–10 µm** long. Possess a **double membrane** — a smooth outer membrane and a highly folded inner membrane forming **cristae**. The matrix inside contains enzymes of the **Krebs (TCA) cycle**, while the cristae house the **Electron Transport Chain (ETC)**.

- **Functions:** Oxidative phosphorylation → generates **~32 ATP** per glucose molecule (vs. 2 ATP from glycolysis).
- **Analogy:** The "power plant" burning fuel to generate electricity (ATP).

> [!NOTE]
> Mitochondria are **semi-autonomous** — they have their own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, and replicate independently. This supports the **endosymbiotic theory**.

### 1.2.3 Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)

A continuous network of flattened sacs (**cisternae**) and tubules connected to the nuclear envelope. Two forms exist:

- **Rough ER (RER):** Studded with ribosomes → synthesizes membrane-bound and secretory proteins.
- **Smooth ER (SER):** No ribosomes → lipid/steroid synthesis, detoxification (liver cells), **Ca²⁺ storage** (muscle cells — sarcoplasmic reticulum).

### 1.2.4 Ribosomes

Tiny granules (**20–30 nm**), composed of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and proteins. Two subunits:
- Eukaryotic: **80S** (60S + 40S) → 50+ proteins + 4 rRNA molecules.
- Prokaryotic: **70S** (50S + 30S).

- **Function:** Site of **translation** (protein synthesis) — links amino acids via peptide bonds.

### 1.2.5 Golgi Apparatus (Post Office)

A stack of **3–10 flattened membrane-bound cisternae** with two distinct faces:
- **Cis face** (forming/entry side) — receives vesicles from ER.
- **Trans face** (maturing/exit side) — dispatches vesicles.

- **Functions:** Protein modification (glycosylation, phosphorylation), sorting, packaging into secretory vesicles.

### 1.2.6 Lysosomes (Suicide Bags)

Membrane-bound sacs containing **~40 hydrolytic enzymes** (proteases, lipases, nucleases, glycosidases) active at **pH ~4.5–5.0** (maintained by H⁺-ATPase pumps).

- **Functions:** **Autophagy** (digesting worn-out organelles), **phagocytosis** (engulfing bacteria), **apoptosis** (programmed cell death — hence "suicide bags").

### 1.2.7 Peroxisomes

Spherical, **0.1–1 µm** diameter, containing **oxidative enzymes** (e.g., catalase, urate oxidase).

- **Functions:** **β-oxidation of very long-chain fatty acids**, detoxification of H₂O₂ into water and oxygen.

### 1.2.8 Cytoskeleton

A network of three protein filament types:

- **Microfilaments (Actin, ~7 nm):** Cell shape, movement, cytokinesis.
- **Intermediate Filaments (~8–12 nm):** Mechanical strength (e.g., keratin in skin, neurofilaments in neurons).
- **Microtubules (Tubulin, ~25 nm):** Organelle transport, mitotic spindle, cilia/flagella structure.

### 1.2.9 Centrosome

Located near the nucleus, contains **two centrioles** arranged perpendicular to each other, each built from **nine triplets of microtubules** (9+0 arrangement).

- **Function:** Organizes the **mitotic spindle** during cell division; forms **basal bodies** for cilia/flagella.

### 1.2.10 Vesicles & Vacuoles

Small membrane-bound sacs for transport (vesicles) or storage/digestion (vacuoles — prominent in plant cells).

---

## 1.3 The Cell Membrane — Structural Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **cell membrane** (plasma membrane) is a **~7–10 nm** thick, selectively permeable barrier enclosing the cell. According to the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (S. J. Singer & G. L. Nicolson, 1972), it is a dynamic, two-dimensional fluid bilayer of **phospholipids** in which a mosaic of **proteins** floats laterally.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Membrane as a Restaurant Bouncer

Think of the cell membrane as a **bouncer at a club's entrance**:
- **Phospholipid bilayer** → The two velvet ropes (hydrophilic heads outside, hydrophobic tails inside) — keeps most things out by default.
- **Channel proteins** → VIP door with a metal detector — only specific shapes/sizes through.
- **Carrier proteins** → Revolving door requiring a key/fingerprint to allow entry.
- **Receptor proteins** → The bouncer's earpiece — receives signals ("passwords") from outside.
- **Cholesterol** → The bouncer's leather jacket — keeps the membrane fluid in cold and stiff in heat.
- **Glycoproteins/glycolipids** → Name tags identifying the cell as "self" to the immune system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **Fluid Mosaic Model** is a guaranteed board question. Always mention **Singer & Nicolson (1972)** and the three key features: (1) lipid bilayer fluidity, (2) protein mosaic, (3) selective permeability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fluid Mosaic Model — Operational Breakdown

The model rests on three foundational principles:

### Principle 1: Lipid Bilayer Fluidity
Phospholipid molecules are **amphipathic** — they possess:
- A **hydrophilic (polar) head**: Phosphate group + glycerol → water-loving.
- **Two hydrophobic (non-polar) tails**: Long **fatty acid chains** (typically **16–18 carbons**) → water-fearing.

In an aqueous environment, they spontaneously self-assemble into a **bilayer** such that heads face the water (extracellular fluid and cytoplasm) and tails hide inside, away from water.

**Fluidity depends on:**
- **Temperature** ↑ → fluidity ↑ (above transition temperature, lipids become liquid).
- **Cholesterol content** → at **high temperatures**, cholesterol restrains movement (decreases fluidity); at **low temperatures**, cholesterol prevents tight packing (increases fluidity). It acts as a **bidirectional fluidity buffer**.
- **Saturation of fatty acid tails** → saturated (no double bonds) → straight → pack tightly → **less fluid**. Unsaturated (cis double bonds create kinks) → **more fluid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The membrane is **asymmetric**:
> - **Outer leaflet:** Predominantly **phosphatidylcholine** and **sphingomyelin** (with glycolipids).
> - **Inner leaflet:** Predominantly **phosphatidylethanolamine**, **phosphatidylserine**, and **phosphatidylinositol**.
> - **Phosphatidylserine** is normally kept in the inner leaflet — its appearance on the outer surface is an **"eat-me" signal** for macrophages (apoptosis).

### Principle 2: The Protein Mosaic
Membrane proteins are classified by their mode of association with the bilayer:

| Type | Location | Mode of Attachment | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Integral (Intrinsic) Proteins** | Embedded in / span the bilayer | **Hydrophobic interactions** with lipid tails | Channel proteins, carriers, receptors, transporters |
| **Peripheral (Extrinsic) Proteins** | Loosely attached to membrane surface | **Weak non-covalent bonds** to integral proteins or polar head groups | Spectrin (RBC cytoskeleton), some enzymes |
| **Lipid-Anchored Proteins** | Covalently attached to a lipid in the bilayer | Covalent bond to a fatty acid or prenyl group | G-proteins, some kinases |

A special class of integral proteins is the **Transmembrane Proteins**, which span the bilayer completely and typically have:
- **α-helical** segments with **hydrophobic amino acid side chains** facing the lipid tails.
- **Hydrophilic loops** projecting into the cytoplasm and extracellular fluid.

### Principle 3: Selective Permeability
The membrane acts as a **gatekeeper**. Permeability ranking (most → least permeable):

> Small non-polar molecules (O₂, CO₂, N₂) > Small uncharged polar (H₂O, ethanol) > Large uncharged polar (glucose) > Ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻)

Ions require **channel or carrier proteins** because their charge cannot cross the hydrophobic core.

---

## 2.2 Functions of the Cell Membrane

1. **Compartmentalization:** Separates intracellular from extracellular environment.
2. **Selective Permeability:** Regulates what enters/leaves the cell.
3. **Cell signaling:** Receptor proteins bind ligands (hormones, neurotransmitters).
4. **Cell adhesion:** Junction proteins (e.g., cadherins, integrins) hold cells together.
5. **Cell recognition:** Glycoproteins/glycolipids act as **antigens** (e.g., ABO blood group antigens).
6. **Anchorage of cytoskeleton:** Provides structural support and links to ECM.
7. **Transport mechanisms:** Diffusion, osmosis, active transport, endocytosis, exocytosis.

---

## 2.3 Transport Across the Membrane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Critical Distinction:**
> Students must clearly differentiate between **Passive** (no ATP, down gradient) and **Active** (requires ATP, against gradient) transport.

### 2.3.1 Passive Transport

| Subtype | Mechanism | Energy | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Diffusion** | Direct passage through bilayer | Kinetic energy | O₂, CO₂ diffusion |
| **Facilitated Diffusion** | Through channel or carrier protein | Kinetic energy | Glucose via GLUT transporters |
| **Osmosis** | Water movement through aquaporins | Kinetic energy | Water reabsorption in kidney |

**Fick's Law of Diffusion** (for the membrane):

$$J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

Where:
- $J$ = net flux (mol/s)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $A$ = membrane surface area (m²)
- $\Delta C$ = concentration difference across membrane (mol/m³)
- $\Delta x$ = membrane thickness (m)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, students should know that **membrane thickness ≈ 7–10 nm**, and that **thinner membranes = faster diffusion**.

### 2.3.2 Active Transport

- **Primary Active Transport:** Uses ATP directly. Example: **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (uses **1 ATP** to pump **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in**, maintaining the resting membrane potential of approximately **−70 mV** inside the cell).
- **Secondary Active Transport (Cotransport):** Uses electrochemical gradient of another ion. Two sub-types:
  - **Symport:** Both molecules move in the **same direction** (e.g., Na⁺-glucose cotransporter in intestinal cells).
  - **Antiport:** Molecules move in **opposite directions** (e.g., Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger).

### 2.3.3 Bulk Transport

- **Endocytosis** (into the cell): Phagocytosis ("cell eating"), Pinocytosis ("cell drinking"), Receptor-mediated endocytosis (clathrin-coated pits).
- **Exocytosis** (out of the cell): Vesicle fuses with plasma membrane via **SNARE proteins**, releasing contents (e.g., neurotransmitter release at synapses).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Membrane Thickness** | $t$ | **7–10 nm** |
| 2 | **Fick's Law** | $J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ | $J$ in mol·s⁻¹ |
| 3 | **Nernst Equation** (ion equilibrium potential) | $E = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ | $E$ in volts |
| 4 | **Nernst (log₁₀ form, 37°C)** | $E = \frac{61}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ | mV |
| 5 | **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage** | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | V |
| 6 | **Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff)** | $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | Pa |
| 7 | **Resting Membrane Potential** | $V_m$ | **≈ −70 mV** |
| 8 | **Na⁺/K⁺ Pump Stoichiometry** | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP | — |
| 9 | **ATP Yield (Full Oxidation)** | 1 Glucose → 32–38 ATP | mol ATP |
| 10 | **Lysosomal pH** | pH | **4.5 – 5.0** |
| 11 | **Mitochondrial Size** | Length × Width | **1–10 µm × 0.5–1 µm** |
| 12 | **Ribosomal Sedimentation (Eukaryote)** | 80S = 60S + 40S | Svedberg units |
| 13 | **Centriole Structure** | 9 triplets of microtubules | 9 + 0 arrangement |
| 14 | **Cilia/Flagella Structure** | 9 doublets + 2 central | 9 + 2 arrangement |
| 15 | **Cytoskeletal Filament Diameters** | Microfilament : Intermediate : Microtubule | **7 nm : 10 nm : 25 nm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU LaTeX Pitfall:** In all KTU answer scripts, write the Nernst equation using **log₁₀** form at body temperature (**310 K / 37°C**), where the constant becomes **61.5 mV** for monovalent ions. Examiners often deduct marks if you leave it in the natural-log form without context.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

- **Drug Delivery:** Liposomes (artificial phospholipid vesicles) exploit membrane fluidity to deliver drugs — applied in cancer chemotherapy (e.g., Doxil).
- **Biosensors:** Membrane-bound enzymes (e.g., glucose oxidase) are immobilized on electrode surfaces for **continuous glucose monitors (CGMs)**.
- **Hemodialysis:** Artificial kidney membranes mimic the **selective permeability** of the glomerulus to filter uremic toxins.
- **Cryopreservation:** Knowledge of lipid phase-transition temperature guides design of cryoprotectants for sperm, embryos, and stem cells.
- **Tissue Engineering:** Scaffolds are designed to mimic the **mechanical and biochemical cues** of cell membranes for cell adhesion and growth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law of Diffusion Applied to Cell Membrane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:**
> Numerical problems on Fick's law, osmotic pressure, and Nernst potential appear routinely in Part A (3-mark) and sometimes in Part B (sub-parts) for **PCBMT205**. The full derivation below equips you to handle both.

### Step 1: Starting Postulate (Empirical Observation)

Adolf Fick (1855) observed that the rate of diffusion across a barrier is **directly proportional** to the **concentration gradient** and the **surface area**, and **inversely proportional** to the **barrier thickness**.

**Step 1 Statement:**
$$J \propto A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

### Step 2: Introduce the Proportionality Constant

The constant of proportionality is the **diffusion coefficient** $D$, which depends on the molecule, the medium, and temperature.

**Step 2 Statement:**
$$J = D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

### Step 3: Add the Directional Sign (Vector Form)

Diffusion occurs from **high → low concentration**, so we insert a **negative sign** (because the gradient $\frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ is negative when $C_{out} > C_{in}$, but the net flux $J$ is defined as positive inward).

**Step 3 Statement:**
$$J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

### Step 4: Normalize to Unit Area (Flux Density $J_d$)

For membrane physiology, we often want flux per unit area, called **flux density** $J_d$ (mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹):

**Step 4 Statement:**
$$J_d = \frac{J}{A} = -D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

### Step 5: Apply to the Plasma Membrane

For a typical cell membrane with thickness $\Delta x = 8 \times 10^{-9}$ m = 8 nm:

$$J_d = -D \cdot \frac{C_{out} - C_{in}}{8 \times 10^{-9}}$$

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Oxygen (O₂) diffuses across an alveolar–capillary membrane. Given:
- $D_{O_2}$ in membrane = $2.1 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s
- $C_{out}$ (alveolus) = $0.20$ mol/m³
- $C_{in}$ (blood) = $0.08$ mol/m³
- Thickness $\Delta x = 1 \times 10^{-8}$ m

**Find:** Flux density $J_d$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 6.1:** Write the formula.

$$J_d = -D \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 6.2:** Compute $\Delta C$.

$$\Delta C = C_{out} - C_{in} = 0.20 - 0.08 = 0.12 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 6.3:** Substitute values.

$$J_d = -(2.1 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot \frac{0.12}{1 \times 10^{-8}}$$

**Step 6.4:** Compute the division.

$$\frac{0.12}{1 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.12 \times 10^{8} = 1.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ mol/m}^4$$

**Step 6.5:** Multiply.

$$J_d = -(2.1 \times 10^{-11}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{7}) = -2.52 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 6.6:** Interpret.

The negative sign confirms flux is **inward** (from alveolus into blood). The magnitude is **2.52 × 10⁻⁴ mol/(m²·s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why the lung has **enormous alveolar surface area (~70 m²)** — to make total transport $J = J_d \times A$ sufficient for whole-body oxygen demand (~250 mL O₂/min at rest).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation for a Single Ion

### Step 1: Start with Electrochemical Equilibrium

At equilibrium, the **chemical driving force** (concentration gradient) exactly balances the **electrical driving force** (membrane potential) for a given ion. No net flux.

### Step 2: Chemical Work for Moving 1 mol of Ions

Work done by concentration gradient (from concentration $C_1$ to $C_2$):

$$W_{chem} = RT \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right)$$

### Step 3: Electrical Work for Moving Charge $zF$ through Potential $E$

$$W_{elec} = z \cdot F \cdot E$$

### Step 4: Set Chemical Work = Electrical Work (Equilibrium)

At equilibrium, these two must be equal in magnitude but opposite in direction:

$$RT \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right) = -z \cdot F \cdot E$$

### Step 5: Solve for $E$ (Nernst Equation)

**Step 5 Statement:**
$$E = -\frac{RT}{zF} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}\right)$$

Equivalently, by reversing the log:

**Step 5 Alternative Form:**
$$E = \frac{RT}{zF} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

### Step 6: Convert to log₁₀ and Insert 310 K (37°C)

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$ and $T = 310$ K:

$$E = \frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot 310}{zF} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}}\right)$$

Substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 6 Statement (Final KTU form):**
$$E \text{ (mV)} = \frac{61.5}{z} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{in}}{[C]_{out}}\right)$$

### Step 7: Numerical Example

**Problem:** For a typical neuron, $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. Calculate $E_K$.

**Step 7.1:** Apply formula with $z = +1$ (K⁺ is monovalent cation).

$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{140}{5}\right)$$

**Step 7.2:** Compute ratio.

$$\frac{140}{5} = 28$$

**Step 7.3:** Compute $\log_{10}(28)$.

$$\log_{10}(28) = \log_{10}(2.8 \times 10) = 1 + \log_{10}(2.8) = 1 + 0.4472 = 1.4472$$

**Step 7.4:** Multiply.

$$E_K = 61.5 \times 1.4472 = 89.0 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 7.5:** Interpret.

The K⁺ equilibrium potential is **+89.0 mV** (inside relative to outside) — but the actual resting potential is **−70 mV**, indicating that K⁺ is the dominant ion but other ions also contribute. The **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation** integrates all permeable ions to give the actual membrane potential.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Organelle vs. Membrane Structure

| Feature | Nucleus | Mitochondria | ER | Golgi | Lysosome | Plasma Membrane |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Membranes** | Double (envelope) | Double (outer + cristae) | Single (continuous) | Single (stacked cisternae) | Single | Single bilayer |
| **Genetic Material** | DNA (linear) | DNA (circular) | None | None | None | None |
| **Ribosomes** | None on envelope | 70S (in matrix) | 80S on RER | None | None | None |
| **Key Function** | Genetic control | ATP production | Protein/lipid synthesis | Modification/packaging | Hydrolysis | Selective barrier |
| **Biomedical Relevance** | Gene therapy | Mitochondrial diseases | Drug detox in liver | Glycosylation disorders | Lysosomal storage diseases | Drug delivery, anesthesia |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A questions are **direct, definition-based, and require concise 3–4 sentence answers**. Aim for **40–60 words per answer**, supplemented with a labelled diagram wherever possible.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six cell organelles and state ONE function of each. (3 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

| # | Organelle | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Nucleus** | Stores DNA and controls cellular activities via transcription. |
| 2 | **Mitochondria** | Produces ATP through oxidative phosphorylation (the "powerhouse"). |
| 3 | **Ribosomes** | Site of protein synthesis (translation) — links amino acids via peptide bonds. |
| 4 | **Endoplasmic Reticulum** | Rough ER synthesizes proteins; Smooth ER synthesizes lipids and detoxifies. |
| 5 | **Golgi Apparatus** | Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for secretion or use within the cell. |
| 6 | **Lysosomes** | Contains hydrolytic enzymes for digestion of waste materials and damaged organelles. |

> **Diagrammatic Requirement:** A neatly labelled diagram of the cell with the organelles marked earns **+1 extra mark** in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Fluid Mosaic Model of the cell membrane. Name its key components. (3 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fluid Mosaic Model**, proposed by **S. J. Singer and G. L. Nicolson in 1972**, describes the cell membrane as a **two-dimensional fluid bilayer of phospholipids** in which a **mosaic of proteins** is embedded.

**Key Components:**
1. **Phospholipid bilayer** (~7–10 nm thick) — forms the basic structural framework.
2. **Integral proteins** — span or partially embed in the bilayer; function as channels, carriers, and receptors.
3. **Peripheral proteins** — loosely attached to the membrane surface; mainly structural or enzymatic.
4. **Cholesterol molecules** — interspersed between phospholipids; regulate membrane fluidity.
5. **Glycoproteins and glycolipids** — carbohydrate chains projecting from the outer surface; function in cell recognition and adhesion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks**, with internal choice. Each question typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. **No sub-part choice is allowed.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Describe in detail the structure of the cell membrane. Add a note on the various transport mechanisms across the membrane.**

#### **Part (a) — Structure of the Cell Membrane (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The cell membrane (plasma membrane) is a **~7–10 nm thick**, selectively permeable barrier that encloses every cell. According to the **Fluid Mosaic Model (Singer & Nicolson, 1972)**, it has the following structural components:

**(1) Phospholipid Bilayer — The Structural Backbone (2 marks):**
Each phospholipid molecule is **amphipathic**, containing a polar **hydrophilic head** (phosphate group + glycerol) and two non-polar **hydrophobic tails** (long fatty acid chains of 16–18 carbons). In an aqueous environment, these self-assemble into a bilayer with heads facing outward (toward water) and tails tucked inward (away from water). The bilayer is **asymmetric** — outer leaflet has phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin; inner leaflet has phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylserine, and phosphatidylinositol.

**(2) Membrane Proteins — The Functional Mosaic (2 marks):**
- **Integral (intrinsic) proteins** are firmly embedded in the bilayer via hydrophobic interactions. **Transmembrane proteins** span the bilayer completely with α-helical hydrophobic segments and hydrophilic loops projecting on either side. Examples: channels, carriers, receptors.
- **Peripheral (extrinsic) proteins** are loosely attached to the membrane surface via non-covalent bonds to integral proteins or polar head groups. Example: spectrin on RBC inner surface.

**(3) Cholesterol — The Fluidity Buffer (1 mark):**
Cholesterol molecules are wedged between phospholipid tails. At **high temperatures**, they **restrain movement (decrease fluidity)**; at **low temperatures**, they **prevent tight packing (increase fluidity)**. This bidirectional action keeps the membrane functional across temperature variations.

**(4) Glycoproteins and Glycolipids (1 mark):**
Carbohydrate chains project from the outer surface, forming the **glycocalyx**. They function in **cell-cell recognition**, adhesion, and as **antigens** (e.g., ABO blood group markers).

**(5) Functions of the Cell Membrane (1 mark):**
- Selective permeability, cell signaling, adhesion, recognition, anchoring cytoskeleton.

**[Diagrammatic Label (must include in script):]**
A labelled diagram of the fluid mosaic model showing: phospholipid bilayer (heads + tails), integral protein, peripheral protein, cholesterol, glycoprotein, glycolipid.

---

**Part (b) — Transport Mechanisms Across the Membrane (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Transport across the plasma membrane is classified into **passive transport** (no ATP, down the gradient) and **active transport** (requires ATP, against the gradient).

**(A) Passive Transport (3 marks):**

| Mechanism | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Diffusion** | Net movement of molecules from high to low concentration directly through the bilayer. Driven by kinetic energy. | O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones |
| **Facilitated Diffusion** | Diffusion assisted by **channel proteins** (form pores, e.g., aquaporins for water, K⁺ channels) or **carrier proteins** (undergo conformational change, e.g., GLUT for glucose). No ATP. | Glucose uptake into RBCs via GLUT1 |
| **Osmosis** | Net movement of **water** across a semipermeable membrane from low solute concentration to high solute concentration. | Water reabsorption in renal tubules |

**Osmotic Pressure** is given by the van't Hoff equation:

$$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$

where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor, $C$ is the molar concentration, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**(B) Active Transport (3 marks):**

- **Primary Active Transport** uses ATP **directly**. The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** hydrolyses 1 ATP to pump **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** against their gradients, maintaining the resting membrane potential of **−70 mV**.
- **Secondary Active Transport (Cotransport)** uses the electrochemical gradient of Na⁺ (established by primary active transport) to drive another molecule. It is of two types:
  - **Symport:** Both molecules move in the **same direction** (e.g., Na⁺-glucose cotransporter SGLT1 in intestinal cells).
  - **Antiport:** Molecules move in **opposite directions** (e.g., Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger in cardiac cells).

**(C) Bulk Transport (1 mark):**
- **Endocytosis** (into cell): Phagocytosis (large particles), Pinocytosis (extracellular fluid), Receptor-mediated endocytosis (specific ligands via clathrin-coated pits).
- **Exocytosis** (out of cell): Vesicles fuse with the plasma membrane via **SNARE proteins** to release contents (e.g., neurotransmitter release at synaptic terminals).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Describe the structure and functions of the following cell organelles: (i) Mitochondria, (ii) Endoplasmic Reticulum, (iii) Golgi Apparatus.**

#### **Part (a) — Mitochondria and Endoplasmic Reticulum (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mitochondria — The Powerhouse of the Cell (4 marks):**

**Structure:**
- Rod-shaped or spherical organelle, **0.5–1.0 µm wide and 1–10 µm long**.
- Bounded by a **double membrane**:
  - **Outer membrane** — smooth, contains porins (permeable to molecules < 5 kDa).
  - **Inner membrane** — highly folded into **cristae** (to increase surface area for the Electron Transport Chain), impermeable, contains cardiolipin.
- **Intermembrane space** — between the two membranes; site of proton (H⁺) accumulation.
- **Matrix** — innermost compartment; contains enzymes of the **Krebs (TCA) cycle**, mitochondrial DNA (circular, ~16,500 bp), 70S ribosomes, and tRNA.

**Functions:**
1. **Oxidative Phosphorylation** — Generates **~32 ATP** per glucose via the Electron Transport Chain and ATP synthase.
2. **Krebs Cycle** — Oxidises acetyl-CoA to CO₂, producing NADH and FADH₂.
3. **β-oxidation of fatty acids** (in liver/muscle).
4. **Heat production** (thermogenesis) via **uncoupling protein-1 (UCP-1)** in brown adipose tissue.
5. **Calcium ion (Ca²⁺) homeostasis** — stores and releases Ca²⁺ to regulate cytoplasmic signalling.
6. **Apoptosis regulation** — releases **cytochrome c** to trigger programmed cell death.

**Biomedical Significance:** Mitochondrial dysfunction is implicated in **Parkinson's disease, mitochondrial myopathies, and aging**. Mitochondrial DNA is maternally inherited.

---

**(ii) Endoplasmic Reticulum (3 marks):**

**Structure:**
- A continuous, single-membrane network of flattened **cisternae** and tubules, continuous with the **outer nuclear membrane**.
- Extends throughout the cytoplasm.
- **Rough ER (RER):** Surface studded with **80S ribosomes**; appears "rough" under electron microscope.
- **Smooth ER (SER):** Lacks ribosomes; appears "smooth."

**Functions:**

| Type | Functions |
|---|---|
| **RER** | (1) Synthesis of membrane-bound and secretory proteins. (2) Initial protein folding and quality control. (3) Glycosylation (addition of N-linked carbohydrates). |
| **SER** | (1) **Lipid and steroid hormone synthesis** (cholesterol, phospholipids, cortisol, estrogen). (2) **Detoxification** of drugs and poisons (esp. in liver hepatocytes — via cytochrome P450). (3) **Ca²⁺ storage** (sarcoplasmic reticulum in muscle). (4) **Gluconeogenesis** (in liver). |

**Biomedical Significance:** Hypertrophy of SER in hepatocytes due to chronic alcohol or barbiturate use increases drug tolerance.

---

#### **Part (b) — Golgi Apparatus and Lysosomes (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Golgi Apparatus — The Cell's Post Office (4 marks):**

**Structure:**
- Discovered by **Camillo Golgi (1898)** using silver staining.
- Comprises **3–10 flattened, stacked membrane-bound cisternae**.
- Two functionally distinct faces:
  - **Cis face** (forming face, convex) — receives transport vesicles from the RER.
  - **Trans face** (maturing face, concave) — dispatches vesicles to lysosomes, plasma membrane, or extracellular space.
- A complex network of tubules and vesicles (**trans-Golgi network, TGN**) connects the stacks.

**Functions:**
1. **Post-translational modification** of proteins: glycosylation (trimming N-linked sugars and adding O-linked sugars), phosphorylation, sulfation, proteolytic cleavage.
2. **Sorting and packaging** proteins into different vesicles based on their final destinations (e.g., lysosomal enzymes get a **mannose-6-phosphate tag** for delivery to lysosomes).
3. **Synthesis of complex polysaccharides** and **sphingolipids**.
4. **Formation of secretory vesicles** that fuse with the plasma membrane to release contents by **exocytosis**.

**Biomedical Significance:** Defects in Golgi trafficking cause **I-cell disease (mucolipidosis II)**, where lysosomal enzymes are wrongly secreted instead of being delivered to lysosomes.

---

**(ii) Lysosomes — The Digestive System of the Cell (3 marks):**

**Structure:**
- Spherical, membrane-bound sacs, **0.1–1.2 µm** in diameter.
- Surrounded by a **single membrane** that contains a **H⁺-ATPase pump** that maintains an internal **acidic pH of 4.5–5.0**.
- Contains **~40 hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes**, including:
  - **Proteases** (cathepsins) — digest proteins.
  - **Lipases** — digest lipids.
  - **Nucleases** — digest DNA/RNA.
  - **Glycosidases** — digest carbohydrates.
  - **Phosphatases** — remove phosphate groups.

**Functions:**
1. **Intracellular digestion** — breaks down macromolecules delivered via endocytosis, phagocytosis, or autophagy.
2. **Autophagy** — engulfs and digests worn-out organelles (e.g., old mitochondria → "mitophagy").
3. **Autolysis** — when the cell is damaged, lysosomes rupture and digest the cell contents → "**suicide bags**" role.
4. **Bone resorption** — osteoclasts release lysosomal enzymes to dissolve bone matrix.
5. **Defense** — phagocytes use lysosomes to destroy engulfed bacteria.

**Biomedical Significance:** Deficiency of a single lysosomal enzyme causes **lysosomal storage diseases**:
- **Tay-Sachs disease** — hexosaminidase A deficiency → GM2 ganglioside accumulation in neurons.
- **Gaucher's disease** — glucocerebrosidase deficiency → glucocerebroside accumulation in macrophages.
- **Pompe disease** — acid α-glucosidase deficiency → glycogen accumulation in lysosomes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Rough vs. Smooth ER:** Many students write that RER synthesizes lipids — this is **wrong**. RER → proteins; SER → lipids. Loses up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the asymmetry of the membrane:** Examiners specifically look for "outer leaflet — phosphatidylcholine" and "inner leaflet — phosphatidylserine." Skipping this loses a **direct 1-mark point**.
> 3. **Confusing 80S and 70S ribosomes:** Eukaryotic cytoplasm has 80S ribosomes; **mitochondria** have 70S ribosomes (a hallmark of their prokaryotic origin). Reversing this is a classic error.
> 4. **Stating "Golgi modifies proteins" without specifying examples** (glycosylation, phosphorylation, sulfation). Examiners expect **specifics**, not vague statements.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase stoichiometry** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) in active transport answers. The **3:2 ratio** is a favourite 1-mark question.
> 6. **Omitting the van't Hoff equation** in osmosis-related questions. Even a symbolic statement of $\pi = iCRT$ earns full credit.
> 7. **Confusing 9+0 (centriole) and 9+2 (cilia/flagella) microtubule arrangements.** This is a high-frequency 1-mark trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-density last-minute revision block. Memorize these bullets the night before the exam.

### Organelles
- [ ] **Nucleus** = largest organelle; houses DNA; surrounded by double-membrane nuclear envelope with **nuclear pores**; **nucleolus** inside for rRNA synthesis.
- [ ] **Mitochondria** = double-membrane; cristae house **ETC**; matrix houses **Krebs cycle**; ~32 ATP/glucose; **70S ribosomes + circular DNA** (endosymbiotic theory).
- [ ] **Rough ER** = ribosome-studded → **protein synthesis, folding, N-glycosylation**.
- [ ] **Smooth ER** = no ribosomes → **lipid/steroid synthesis, detoxification (cytochrome P450), Ca²⁺ storage**.
- [ ] **Ribosomes** = 80S (eukaryote) = 60S + 40S; site of **translation**.
- [ ] **Golgi Apparatus** = cis face (entry from ER) → trans face (exit via vesicles); **modifies, sorts, packages**; **mannose-6-phosphate tag** targets enzymes to lysosomes.
- [ ] **Lysosomes** = "**suicide bags**"; pH **4.5–5.0**; contain hydrolytic enzymes; **autophagy, autolysis, phagocytosis**.
- [ ] **Peroxisomes** = catalase breaks down H₂O₂; **β-oxidation of very long-chain fatty acids**.
- [ ] **Cytoskeleton** = microfilaments (actin, **7 nm**) + intermediate filaments (**~10 nm**) + microtubules (tubulin, **25 nm**).
- [ ] **Centrosome** = 2 centrioles with **9+0** microtubule arrangement; organizes mitotic spindle.
- [ ] **Cilia/Flagella** = **9+2** arrangement; basal body derived from centriole; powered by **dynein** motor protein.

### Cell Membrane
- [ ] **Fluid Mosaic Model** = **Singer & Nicolson, 1972**.
- [ ] **Thickness** = **7–10 nm**.
- [ ] **Phospholipid bilayer** = amphipathic; hydrophilic heads outside, hydrophobic tails inside; **asymmetric** leaflets.
- [ ] **Integral proteins** = span bilayer; **transmembrane α-helices**; channels, carriers, receptors.
- [ ] **Peripheral proteins** = loosely surface-attached; non-covalent bonds.
- [ ] **Cholesterol** = bidirectional fluidity buffer.
- [ ] **Glycoproteins/glycolipids** = glycocalyx; cell recognition; ABO blood group antigens.
- [ ] **Fick's Law** = $J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation (37°C)** = $E (\text{mV}) = \frac{61.5}{z} \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{in}}{[C]_{out}}\right)$.
- [ ] **Resting membrane potential** = **−70 mV**; maintained by **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in, 1 ATP).
- [ ] **Van't Hoff osmotic pressure** = $\pi = iCRT$.

### High-Yield KTU Buzzwords (use these exact terms in answers)
- "**Powerhouse of the cell**" → Mitochondria.
- "**Suicide bags**" → Lysosomes.
- "**Post office / Shipping department**" → Golgi apparatus.
- "**Library + CEO's office**" → Nucleus.
- "**Bouncer / security gate**" → Plasma membrane.
- "**Fluid mosaic**" → Singer & Nicolson, 1972.
- "**Selective permeability**" → Plasma membrane function.
- "**Endosymbiotic theory**" → Mitochondrial origin (Lynn Margulis, 1967).

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cell Membrane: The Biological Gateway

The **plasma membrane** (also called the *cell membrane* or *plasmalemma*) is a **~7–10 nm thick**, amphipathic, selectively permeable phospholipid bilayer embedded with proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrate moieties. It separates the **intracellular fluid (ICF)** from the **extracellular fluid (ECF)** and governs *every* exchange of matter, energy, and information between the cell and its environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The membrane is **selectively permeable** — not *freely* permeable. This selectivity is the foundation for the four transport modes studied in this module: Passive Diffusion, Facilitated Diffusion, Primary Active Transport, and Secondary Active Transport.

The structural model universally accepted is the **Fluid Mosaic Model** (Singer & Nicolson, 1972). The membrane's lipid core is **hydrophobic**, so transport behavior depends critically on the **solubility of the solute in lipids**, its **size**, its **charge**, and the presence of **specific carrier proteins** or **channel proteins**.

---

## 1.2 The Four Major Transport Modes — At a Glance

| # | Mode | Energy Source | Carrier / Channel | Direction w.r.t. Gradient |
|---|------|---------------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Passive Diffusion** | None (down gradient) | None | High → Low concentration |
| 2 | **Facilitated Diffusion** | None (down gradient) | Channel / Carrier protein | High → Low concentration |
| 3 | **Primary Active Transport** | **ATP hydrolysis** | Pump protein (e.g., Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) | Low → High (against gradient) |
| 4 | **Secondary Active Transport** | Electrochemical gradient of another ion (often Na⁺) | Symporter / Antiporter | Low → High (against own gradient) |

---

## 1.3 Passive Diffusion — The "Spill-Over" Mechanism

### Formal Definition
**Passive Diffusion** is the *net, spontaneous* movement of solute molecules across a permeable membrane **down their electrochemical gradient** without the assistance of membrane proteins and **without the expenditure of metabolic energy (ATP)**. It is governed purely by the Second Law of Thermodynamics — the system moves toward maximum entropy.

### Intuitive Analogy 🪂
> Imagine opening a window in a *hot, stuffy room*. The smell of coffee drifts out, and fresh air drifts in — **without you pushing anything**. Molecules bounce, collide, and statistically drift from where they are *crowded* to where they are *empty*. That is passive diffusion. The cell does no work; physics does all the work.

### Key Characteristics
- **Non-saturable** — flux increases linearly with concentration gradient.
- **No stereospecificity** — any lipid-soluble molecule can traverse.
- **No competitive inhibition** — molecules do not "fight" for slots.
- **Temperature dependent** — follows the Arrhenius relationship; **Q₁₀ ≈ 1.2–1.5** (chemical, not conformational).
- **Membrane-crossing rule of thumb:** *Smaller, nonpolar (lipophilic), uncharged* molecules diffuse fastest. Permeability order for a typical lipid bilayer: **O₂, CO₂, H₂O, steroid hormones > urea > glycerol > glucose > Cl⁻, K⁺, Na⁺ (essentially zero)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lipid Solubility Rule (Overton's Rule):** The permeability coefficient of a solute is directly proportional to its **oil–water partition coefficient**. Hydrophobic drugs (e.g., anesthetics) cross membranes readily; charged ions almost never do — unless a protein helps.

---

## 1.4 Facilitated Diffusion — The "Turnstile" Mechanism

### Formal Definition
**Facilitated Diffusion** is the carrier-mediated or channel-mediated transport of specific solutes **down their electrochemical gradient** through transmembrane **carrier proteins** or **channel proteins**. It is *passive* (no ATP) but *facilitated* (requires a protein), and exhibits properties of **saturation kinetics** identical to enzymes (Michaelis–Menten behavior).

### Intuitive Analogy 🚪
> Think of a *crowded concert hall with a single, locked door*. People (molecules) want to leave, but the door only opens for those with a *valid ticket* (molecular recognition). When the hall is half-empty, the door handles traffic easily. But once the hall is *packed*, the door becomes a bottleneck — adding more people does not make them leave faster. The door (channel/carrier) is **saturated**. This is *saturation kinetics*, the hallmark of facilitated diffusion.

### Sub-Types
- **Channel-mediated:** Forms a continuous aqueous pore. Examples: **Aquaporins** (water), **Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** (nerve impulse), **Ligand-gated ion channels** (e.g., ACh receptor). Transport is very fast (10⁶–10⁸ ions/sec).
- **Carrier-mediated:** The protein undergoes a **conformational change** to shuttle the solute. Slower (10²–10⁴ molecules/sec). Examples: **GLUT transporters** for glucose.

### Hallmark Properties
1. **Saturation kinetics** — follows Michaelis–Menten equation: $J = \dfrac{J_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$
2. **Stereochemical specificity** — D-glucose is transported, but L-glucose is not.
3. **Competitive inhibition** — structurally similar molecules compete for the same carrier.
4. **Temperature sensitive** — **Q₁₀ ≈ 2–3** (conformational change, not simple diffusion).

---

## 1.5 Primary Active Transport — The "Powered Pump" (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase)

### Formal Definition
**Primary Active Transport** is the movement of a solute **against its electrochemical gradient**, powered *directly* by the **hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate (ATP)**. The membrane proteins that catalyze this are called **pumps** or **ATPases** (P-type, V-type, F-type, ABC transporters).

### Intuitive Analogy 🔋
> A **sodium–potassium pump** is like a *battery-powered revolving door* in a building. The revolving door **forces** people to enter the building on the 5th floor and exit on the ground floor — *against the natural flow* (which would be people drifting downhill, not uphill). The "battery" is **ATP**, and the revolving door is the **α-subunit of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**. Without battery power, the door stops and ions equilibrate.

### The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase — The Master Pump
For every **1 molecule of ATP hydrolyzed**, the pump exports **3 Na⁺ out** and imports **2 K⁺ in**.
- Stoichiometry: **3 Na⁺ : 2 K⁺ : 1 ATP**
- Net effect: **1 positive charge moved OUT per cycle** → the pump is **electrogenic**.
- Maintains: **Intracellular [K⁺] ≈ 140 mM, [Na⁺] ≈ 10–15 mM**; **Extracellular [Na⁺] ≈ 145 mM, [K⁺] ≈ 4–5 mM**.
- Accounts for **~25–40% of all ATP consumed** in a typical human at rest (in the brain and kidney, it can be **>60%**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The Na⁺/K⁺ pump is **electrogenic** because it moves a *net* of +1 charge out of the cell per cycle. This contributes directly to the **resting membrane potential** of approximately **−70 mV** (inside negative).

---

## 1.6 Secondary Active Transport — The "Hitchhiker" Mechanism

### Formal Definition
**Secondary Active Transport** uses the **electrochemical gradient of an ion (typically Na⁺)**, established by a primary active transporter, as its *energy source* to move a *second* solute **against its own gradient**. The Na⁺ ion moves *down* its gradient (exergonic), and this energy is *coupled* to move the other solute *up* its gradient (endergonic). **No ATP is hydrolyzed by the secondary transporter itself.**

### Intuitive Analogy 🪂💨
> Imagine a *skydiver (Na⁺) jumping out of a plane*. As they fall, they grab a sack of mail (glucose) and carry it *upward* into a high-altitude balloon (the cell interior). The skydiver's *own* downward fall powers the *upward* delivery of the mail. The plane (Na⁺/K⁺ pump) re-charges the skydiver at the bottom; the skydiver does the heavy lifting. ATP is used *only* by the pump, *not* by the secondary transporter itself.

### Two Sub-Types

| Sub-type | Geometry | Example |
|----------|----------|---------|
| **Co-Transport (Symport)** | Both ions move in the **same direction** across the membrane | **SGLT1** (Na⁺–glucose cotransporter in intestinal epithelium) |
| **Counter-Transport (Antiport)** | The two ions move in **opposite directions** | **Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX)** in cardiac myocytes; **Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger (NHE)** in kidney |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** In **symport**, the driving ion and the driven solute travel *together* (e.g., Na⁺ + glucose → both into cell). In **antiport**, they *exchange* (e.g., Na⁺ in, Ca²⁺ out). The driving ion always travels *down* its gradient.

---

## 1.7 Summary Schematic of the Four Modes

```
       ECF                    ICF
   (Extracellular)        (Intracellular)
   High Na+, Low K+       Low Na+, High K+

   ┌─────────── MEMBRANE ───────────┐
   │  1. Passive Diffusion:  CO2,O2 │   (no protein, down [ ])
   │  2. Facilitated Diff.:  GLUT   │   (carrier, down [ ])
   │  3. Primary Active:    Na/K    │   (pump, ATP, against [ ])
   │  4. Secondary Active:  SGLT1   │   (symport, against [ ])
   │                  NCX (antiport)│
   └────────────────────────────────┘
```

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Passive Diffusion — Quantitative Framework

### Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)

For steady-state diffusion across a membrane of thickness $L$ and area $A$:

$$J = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{L}$$

where:
- $J$ = net molar flux (mol/s)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $A$ = membrane area (m²)
- $\Delta C$ = concentration difference across the membrane (mol/m³)
- $L$ = membrane thickness (m)
- The **negative sign** indicates flux flows from *high* to *low* concentration.

### Fick's Second Law (Time-Dependent)

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

This is the foundation of all pharmacokinetic models (drug distribution in tissue) and explains why small ions take time to reach equilibrium.

### Permeability Coefficient

$$P = \frac{D}{L} \quad \left[\text{m/s}\right]$$

So flux can be compactly written as:

$$J = P \cdot A \cdot \Delta C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight (Biomedical Engineering relevance):** The same Fick's law governs **oxygen transport from capillaries to tissue** and **drug diffusion from transdermal patches**. The Fick's-law framework is the most-tested concept in transport physiology.

### Diffusion Coefficient (Stokes–Einstein Equation)

For a spherical particle in a viscous medium:

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$$

where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, $T$ is absolute temperature, $\eta$ is viscosity, and $r$ is the hydrodynamic radius. This explains *why small molecules diffuse faster than large ones*.

### Graham's Law (Gases)

For gas-phase diffusion:

$$\frac{r_1}{r_2} = \sqrt{\frac{M_2}{M_1}}$$

(Used for understanding pulmonary gas exchange and anesthesia kinetics.)

---

## 2.2 Facilitated Diffusion — Michaelis–Menten Kinetics

Because carriers have a finite number of binding sites, transport **saturates** at high substrate concentrations. The flux equation is identical in form to enzyme kinetics:

$$J = \frac{J_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

where:
- $J_{\max}$ = maximum flux (when all carriers are occupied)
- $K_m$ = Michaelis constant = substrate concentration at $J = J_{\max}/2$ (a measure of *affinity*; **low $K_m$ = high affinity**)
- $[S]$ = extracellular substrate concentration

### Lineweaver–Burk Linearization (for experimental plot)

$$\frac{1}{J} = \frac{K_m}{J_{\max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{J_{\max}}$$

Plotting $1/J$ vs. $1/[S]$ gives a straight line with slope $K_m/J_{\max}$ and y-intercept $1/J_{\max}$. This is the same plot used in enzymology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Passive diffusion is a straight line through the origin** (linear, $J \propto [S]$, no saturation). **Facilitated diffusion is a hyperbola** (saturable). This single distinction is a common KTU question.

---

## 2.3 Electrochemical Gradients & the Nernst Equation

A charged ion experiences *two* driving forces:
1. **Chemical gradient** (concentration difference)
2. **Electrical gradient** (membrane potential)

The Nernst equation gives the **equilibrium potential** (the voltage at which these two forces exactly balance) for a single ion:

$$E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \cdot \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

Converting to log₁₀ and using body temperature (37 °C, $T = 310$ K):

$$E_{\text{ion}} \approx \frac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \cdot \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}$$

For typical mammalian cells:

| Ion | $[C]_{\text{out}}$ (mM) | $[C]_{\text{in}}$ (mM) | $z$ | $E_{\text{ion}}$ (mV) |
|-----|-----------------------|----------------------|-----|---------------------|
| Na⁺ | 145 | 12 | +1 | **+67** |
| K⁺ | 4 | 155 | +1 | **−98** |
| Cl⁻ | 120 | 4 | −1 | **−90** |
| Ca²⁺ | 1.2 | 10⁻⁴ | +2 | **+129** |

### Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

For a membrane permeable to multiple ions simultaneously, the **resting membrane potential** is given by:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{in}}}{P_K[K^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{out}}}$$

At 37 °C:

$$V_m \approx 61 \text{ mV} \cdot \log_{10} \frac{P_K[K^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{in}}}{P_K[K^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{out}}}$$

This yields $V_m \approx -70$ mV in a typical neuron, dominated by $K^+$ permeability at rest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** The resting membrane potential is *closer to* $E_K$ than to $E_{Na}$ because the membrane at rest is **~25–100× more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺** (i.e., $P_K \gg P_{Na}$). The Na⁺/K⁺ pump sustains the gradients, but the *permeability ratio* determines the voltage.

---

## 2.4 Primary Active Transport — Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Equations

### Free Energy Available from the Na⁺ Gradient

The energy stored in the Na⁺ gradient can be computed from the Nernst equation and is the energy "currency" used by secondary transporters:

$$\Delta G_{Na} = RT \cdot \ln \frac{[Na^+]_{\text{in}}}{[Na^+]_{\text{out}}}$$

For typical values ($\Delta V_m = -70$ mV):

$$\Delta G_{Na} = zF V_m + RT \ln \frac{[Na^+]_{\text{in}}}{[Na^+]_{\text{out}}}$$

At 37 °C with $[Na^+]_{\text{in}} = 12$ mM, $[Na^+]_{\text{out}} = 145$ mM, $V_m = -70$ mV:

$$\Delta G_{Na} \approx -11.5 \text{ kJ/mol per Na}^+ \text{ ion entering}$$

### Energy from ATP Hydrolysis

$$\text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{ADP} + P_i \quad ; \quad \Delta G_{\text{ATP}} \approx -50 \text{ to } -60 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The Na⁺/K⁺ pump uses ~30–40 kJ per cycle, leaving the rest as thermal dissipation. **The 3:2 stoichiometry of Na⁺:K⁺ is *not* electrically neutral** — net one positive charge is exported per cycle.

### Pump Current

The steady-state electrogenic current is:

$$I_{pump} = n \cdot F \cdot \Phi_{pump}$$

where $n$ is the net charge moved per cycle (= 1) and $\Phi_{pump}$ is the pump turnover rate (mol/s).

---

## 2.5 Secondary Active Transport — Coupling Equations

The free energy for a coupled Na⁺-solute symporter (e.g., SGLT1 moving 1 glucose with 1 Na⁺):

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} = \Delta G_{Na} + \Delta G_{\text{glucose}}$$

For transport to be thermodynamically favorable:

$$\Delta G_{\text{total}} < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta G_{\text{glucose}} < -\Delta G_{Na}$$

This sets the **maximum concentration ratio** of glucose that can be accumulated:

$$[\text{glucose}]_{\text{in}} / [\text{glucose}]_{\text{out}} < \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta G_{Na}}{RT}\right)$$

For SGLT1 in the intestinal epithelium, glucose can be concentrated **~300–1000×** across the brush-border membrane.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | What it Computes | Key Variables | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $J = -D \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{L}$ | Steady-state diffusive flux | $D, A, \Delta C, L$ | mol/s |
| 2 | $P = D/L$ | Permeability coefficient | $D, L$ | m/s |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \eta r}$ | Diffusion coeff. (Stokes–Einstein) | $T, \eta, r$ | m²/s |
| 4 | $E_{\text{ion}} \approx \dfrac{61 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$ | Equilibrium potential | $z, [C]$ | mV |
| 5 | GHK equation | Resting $V_m$ | $P_{\text{ion}}, [C]$ | mV; ≈ **−70 mV** |
| 6 | $J = \dfrac{J_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Facilitated-diffusion flux | $J_{\max}, K_m$ | mol/s |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{J} = \dfrac{K_m}{J_{\max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{J_{\max}}$ | Lineweaver–Burk | linear plot | — |
| 8 | $\Delta G = zFV_m + RT \ln \dfrac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}$ | Energy from ion gradient | $z, V_m, [C]$ | J/mol |
| 9 | 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP | Pump stoichiometry | — | electrogenic |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{\text{ATP}} \approx -50$ to $-60$ kJ/mol | ATP hydrolysis energy | — | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Memorize formulas (1), (4), (5), (6), and (9) first. These alone cover 80% of past exam questions on this module.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

| Phenomenon | Underlying Transport | Engineering Application |
|------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **O₂ delivery from alveoli to blood** | Passive diffusion (Fick's law) | Design of **artificial lungs (ECMO)**, ventilators |
| **Glucose uptake by muscle** | Facilitated diffusion (GLUT4) | **Insulin pump** design, continuous glucose monitors |
| **Nerve impulse** | Voltage-gated Na⁺/K⁺ channels + Na⁺/K⁺ pump | **Neural prosthetics**, deep brain stimulators |
| **Cardiac contraction** | Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (antiport) | **ECG interpretation**, defibrillator design |
| **Intestinal glucose absorption** | SGLT1 symport | **Oral rehydration therapy** (Na⁺ + glucose co-transport) |
| **Renal reabsorption** | Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger, Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ symport | **Dialysis machine** design, diuretics pharmacology |
| **Drug absorption across skin** | Passive diffusion | **Transdermal patches** (nicotine, fentanyl, scopolamine) |
