## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define heart sounds. Explain the mechanism of production of S1 and S2.**

**Model Answer:**

**Heart sounds** are the audible mechanical vibrations produced by the closure of heart valves and the resulting turbulence of blood flow during the cardiac cycle. They are characterized by low amplitude (0.1–1 mV at the chest surface) and low frequency (20–1000 Hz) and are recorded graphically using a **Phonocardiograph (PCG)**.

**Mechanism of S1 ("Lub"):**
S1 is produced by the **closure of the atrioventricular (AV) valves — the Mitral and Tricuspid valves** at the beginning of ventricular systole. The sequence is:
1. Atrial systole completes ventricular filling.
2. Ventricular depolarization (QRS complex) triggers ventricular contraction.
3. Ventricular pressure rises rapidly and exceeds atrial pressure.
4. This pressure reversal slams the mitral and tricuspid leaflets shut in approximately 0.02 seconds.
5. The sudden deceleration of blood and vibration of the valve leaflets, chordae tendineae, and ventricular wall produce the audible "lub" sound. **Frequency: 30–70 Hz. Duration: ~0.14 s.**

**Mechanism of S2 ("Dub"):**
S2 is produced by the **closure of the semilunar valves — the Aortic and Pulmonary valves** at the end of ventricular systole. The sequence is:
1. Ventricular systole ends; ventricles begin to relax.
2. Ventricular pressure falls below the pressure in the aorta and pulmonary artery.
3. The aortic and pulmonary valves snap shut, trapping blood in the great arteries.
4. The closed valve leaflets vibrate, producing the "dub" sound. **Frequency: 60–100 Hz. Duration: ~0.11 s.**

**Physiological splitting of S2** occurs during inspiration, when A2 precedes P2 by 40–60 ms, due to delayed right ventricular ejection from increased venous return.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a heart murmur? Explain the Levine grading scale.**

**Model Answer:**

A **heart murmur** is an abnormal, sustained "whooshing," "swishing," or "rasping" sound produced during the cardiac cycle as a result of **turbulent (non-laminar) blood flow** through the heart's chambers, valves, or great vessels. Turbulence occurs when the **Reynolds number exceeds ~2000**, creating vibrations in the surrounding cardiac structures and blood. Murmurs are characterized by their timing (systolic, diastolic, continuous), shape (crescendo-decrescendo, plateau), pitch, quality, location, and radiation.

**The Levine Intensity Grading Scale** is a clinical tool used to grade the loudness of a murmur from 1 to 6:

| Grade | Description | Thrill |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| **Grade 1/6** | Very faint, heard only after careful, prolonged auscultation | Absent |
| **Grade 2/6** | Soft, but immediately audible on placing the stethoscope | Absent |
| **Grade 3/6** | Moderately loud, no palpable thrill | Absent |
| **Grade 4/6** | Loud; a palpable thrill is present | Present |
| **Grade 5/6** | Very loud; thrill present; audible with only one edge of the stethoscope | Strong |
| **Grade 6/6** | Extremely loud; heard even without the stethoscope on the chest | Strong |

> Grades 1–2 are typically **innocent/functional** murmurs (especially in children), while **grades 4–6 are almost always pathological** and warrant further investigation (e.g., echocardiography).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the mechanism, timing, and clinical significance of S3 and S4 heart sounds. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between systolic and diastolic murmurs with suitable examples. Explain the characteristic features of mitral stenosis and aortic stenosis murmurs. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) S3 and S4 Heart Sounds:**

**S3 — The Third Heart Sound (Ventricular Gallop):**

**Mechanism:** S3 is produced during the **rapid ventricular filling phase of early diastole**, approximately 0.12–0.18 seconds after S2. As blood rushes from the atria into the ventricles, the **sudden deceleration of blood against the ventricular wall** causes vibration. In a normal, compliant ventricle, this energy is absorbed silently. However, in a **dilated, volume-overloaded ventricle**, the wall vibrates audibly because of increased mass and reduced compliance.

**Timing:** Occurs in early diastole, 0.12–0.18 s after S2. Best heard at the **apex** with the **bell of the stethoscope**, in the **left lateral decubitus position**.

**Frequency:** Low-pitched, **25–50 Hz**.

**Cadence:** "Ken-tuc-y" — the "y" represents S3.

**Clinical Significance:**
- **Physiological S3:** Normal in children, adolescents, pregnant women, and well-conditioned athletes due to a thin, highly compliant chest wall and vigorous early diastolic filling.
- **Pathological S3:** In adults, it indicates **ventricular volume overload or heart failure** (e.g., congestive heart failure, mitral regurgitation, aortic regurgitation, dilated cardiomyopathy). S3 in an adult is a **poor prognostic marker** and is associated with increased mortality in heart failure patients.

**S4 — The Fourth Heart Sound (Atrial Gallop):**

**Mechanism:** S4 is produced during **late diastole, just before S1**, coinciding with **atrial contraction (atrial kick)**. When the atria contract to top off the ventricles, blood is forcefully ejected into a **stiff, non-compliant ventricle**. The ventricle wall vibrates as it resists this final filling.

**Timing:** Occurs in late diastole, immediately preceding S1. Best heard at the **apex** with the **bell**, in the **left lateral decubitus position**.

**Frequency:** Very low-pitched, **20–40 Hz**.

**Cadence:** "Ten-nes-see" — the "Ten" represents S4.

**Clinical Significance:**
- S4 is **always pathological in adults** (never physiological).
- Indicates **decreased ventricular compliance** due to:
  - Left ventricular hypertrophy (from systemic hypertension)
  - Aortic stenosis (pressure-overloaded, hypertrophied LV)
  - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
  - Ischemic heart disease / myocardial infarction (stiff, scarred myocardium)
- **Absent in atrial fibrillation** (no organized atrial contraction to produce the atrial kick).

**(b) Systolic vs. Diastolic Murmurs:**

| Feature | Systolic Murmurs | Diastolic Murmurs |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Timing** | Between S1 and S2 | Between S2 and next S1 |
| **Mechanism** | Flow across stenotic semilunar valves OR backflow through incompetent AV valves | Flow back through incompetent semilunar valves OR forward across stenotic AV valves |
| **Common causes** | Aortic stenosis, pulmonary stenosis, MR, TR, VSD, HCM | Aortic regurgitation, pulmonary regurgitation, MS, TS |
| **Intensity grading** | Levine 1–6 | Levine 1–4 (usually softer) |

**Mitral Stenosis Murmur:**
- **Type:** Mid-diastolic rumble with **presystolic accentuation** (in sinus rhythm).
- **Quality:** Low-pitched, rumbling, best heard with the **bell** at the **apex** in the **left lateral decubitus position**.
- **Associated findings:** Loud S1 (leaflets snap shut from a wide-open position), **opening snap** (pliable valve suddenly halts its opening), presystolic accentuation (atrial contraction forces blood through narrowed valve).
- **Pathophysiology:** Narrowed mitral orifice (normal area 4–6 cm² reduced to <1.5 cm²) obstructs flow from left atrium to left ventricle during diastole, raising left atrial pressure (and consequently pulmonary venous pressure → dyspnea).

**Aortic Stenosis Murmur:**
- **Type:** Mid-systolic **crescendo-decrescendo (diamond-shaped) ejection murmur**.
- **Quality:** Harsh, rough, high-pitched, best heard with the **diaphragm** at the **right 2nd intercostal space (aortic area)**, **radiating to the carotids**.
- **Associated findings:** Soft or absent S2 (A2 component diminished due to immobile calcified valve), S4 (LV hypertrophy), pulsus parvus et tardus (small and delayed carotid pulse).
- **Pathophysiology:** Calcified, narrowed aortic valve obstructs LV outflow during systole, increasing LV pressure and generating high-velocity turbulent flow across the stenosis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the phonocardiographic (PCG) recording of heart sounds and their correlation with the ECG. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the physiological splitting of S2. Discuss the clinical significance of abnormal splitting of S2. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Phonocardiographic Recording and ECG Correlation:**

A **Phonocardiogram (PCG)** is the graphical recording of heart sounds and murmurs, displayed as amplitude vs. time, typically synchronized with an **ECG** for temporal reference.

**Recording Setup:**
- **Transducer:** Electronic stethoscope, piezoelectric microphone, or accelerometer placed on the chest wall at one of the four classical auscultation areas:
  - Aortic area (right 2nd intercostal space)
  - Pulmonary area (left 2nd intercostal space)
  - Tricuspid area (lower left sternal border)
  - Mitral area (apex, 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line)
- **Signal conditioning:** Low-noise preamplifier (gain ~1000×) → bandpass filter (20–1000 Hz) → 50 Hz notch filter → ADC (≥2000 Hz sampling) → display.

**PCG Signal Characteristics:**

| Sound | Frequency (Hz) | Duration (s) | Amplitude | Timing vs. ECG |
|-------|----------------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| **S1** | 30–70 | 0.14 | Medium | Coincides with QRS (0.02–0.04 s after Q) |
| **S2** | 60–100 | 0.11 | Loudest | Coincides with end of T wave |
| **S3** | 25–50 | 0.05 | Low | 0.12–0.18 s after S2 |
| **S4** | 20–40 | 0.04 | Low | Just before QRS / P-Q interval |

**Typical Tracing (using a flowchart-style textual diagram):**

```
ECG:   _____|^^^^|_________________^^^^^|________________|^^^^|______________
              QRS                          T                   QRS
PCG:   _________|M1_T1|_____|A2_P2|_|S3|____|S4|____________|M1_T1|________
                    S1            S2                    S1
                   (Lub)         (Dub)                 (Lub)
```

**Engineering Significance:** Modern PCG systems use **digital signal processing (DSP)** — particularly **Wavelet Transform (WT)** and **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** — to denoise the signal and automatically classify murmurs. Deep learning models (CNN, LSTM) trained on large PCG databases (e.g., PhysioNet) can detect valvular pathologies with high accuracy, supporting **telemedicine and remote cardiac screening**.

**(b) Physiological Splitting of S2:**

S2 is produced by the near-simultaneous closure of the **Aortic (A2)** and **Pulmonary (P2)** semilunar valves. A2 normally precedes P2 by a small, variable interval.

**Mechanism of Physiological Splitting:**

The A2–P2 interval varies with the **respiratory cycle**:

- **During Expiration:** Venous return to both sides of the heart is balanced. Right and left ventricular ejection times are nearly equal. A2 precedes P2 by only **~30 ms** — often not perceptible to the human ear (a "single" S2 is heard).

- **During Inspiration:** 
  1. **Intrathoracic pressure drops** (becomes more negative).
  2. This **increases venous return to the right heart**, prolonging right ventricular ejection time → P2 is **delayed**.
  3. Simultaneously, **pulmonary vascular capacitance increases**, slightly reducing left-sided venous return → A2 occurs **earlier**.
  4. The net effect is a **widened A2–P2 interval of 40–60 ms**, audible as a "split" S2.
  5. This is **physiological splitting** — a **normal variant**.

**Abnormal Splitting of S2 — Clinical Significance:**

| Pattern | Description | Clinical Causes | Significance |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Wide, Fixed Splitting** | A2–P2 split is widened (>60 ms) and does NOT vary with respiration | **Atrial Septal Defect (ASD)** | Left-to-right shunt continuously overloads RV, prolonging RV ejection regardless of respiration |
| **Paradoxical (Reversed) Splitting** | P2 precedes A2 (split heard on EXPIRATION, narrows on inspiration) | **LBBB, Severe Aortic Stenosis, HOCM** | Delayed LV activation/ejection → A2 is delayed, occurring AFTER P2 |
| **Single S2** | Only A2 or only P2 audible; no splitting | **Tetralogy of Fallot, Severe Aortic Stenosis, Truncus Arteriosus** | One component is inaudible (soft P2 in ToF; soft A2 in AS) |
| **Accentuated P2** | Loud P2 | **Pulmonary Hypertension** | High pulmonary artery pressure slams the pulmonary valve shut more forcefully |
| **Accentuated A2** | Loud A2 | **Systemic Hypertension** | High systemic arterial pressure |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the order of A2 and P2:** A2 always precedes P2 in normal hearts. Stating "P2 precedes A2" in the normal cardiac cycle is a common error that costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the respiratory variation:** The definition of *physiological* splitting specifically includes respiratory variation. Stating that S2 "splits during inspiration" without mentioning the **expiratory single S2** loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing paradoxical splitting with wide fixed splitting:** Paradoxical splitting NARROWS on inspiration (opposite of physiological); wide fixed splitting does NOT vary with respiration. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Levine grading scale** when asked about murmur intensity — even if not explicitly asked, adding it shows depth.
> 5. **Mixing up the timings of S3 and S4:** S3 is in EARLY diastole (after S2); S4 is in LATE diastole (just before S1). Reversing these is a critical error.
> 6. **Failing to connect murmurs to Reynolds number / fluid dynamics:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes engineering-physiology integration. Stating "turbulent flow causes murmurs" without invoking the Reynolds number ($Re > 2000$) loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Heart Sounds & Murmurs:**

- **S1 ("Lub"):** Closure of **mitral + tricuspid** valves. Marks the **start of systole**. Frequency **30–70 Hz**, duration **~0.14 s**. Correlates with the **QRS complex** of ECG.
- **S2 ("Dub"):** Closure of **aortic + pulmonary** valves. Marks the **end of systole**. Frequency **60–100 Hz**, duration **~0.11 s**. Correlates with the **end of the T wave** of ECG.
- **S3 (Ventricular Gallop):** Early diastole, **0.12–0.18 s after S2**. Cause: rapid ventricular filling against a **dilated** ventricle. Frequency **25–50 Hz**. Physiological in children; pathological in adults (CHF).
- **S4 (Atrial Gallop):** Late diastole, **just before S1**. Cause: atrial contraction against a **stiff, non-compliant** ventricle. Frequency **20–40 Hz**. **Always pathological** in adults (LVH, AS, IHD).
- **S2 Splitting — Expiration:** A2–P2 ≈ **30 ms** (often single).
- **S2 Splitting — Inspiration:** A2–P2 ≈ **40–60 ms** (physiological split).
- **Wide fixed split S2 → ASD.**
- **Paradoxical (reversed) split S2 → LBBB, severe AS.**
- **Single S2 → Tetralogy of Fallot, severe AS.**
- **Murmurs** = sustained vibrations from **turbulent flow ($Re > 2000$)** through abnormal valves or septal defects.
- **Systolic murmurs:** Mid-systolic ejection (AS, PS), Holosystolic (MR, TR, VSD), Late systolic (MVP).
- **Diastolic murmurs:** Early diastolic regurgitation (AR, PR), Mid-diastolic rumble (MS), Late diastolic presystolic (MS with sinus rhythm).
- **Continuous murmur:** Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA) — "machinery murmur."
- **Levine Scale:** 1/6 (faintest) to 6/6 (audible without stethoscope). Grades 4–6 = pathological.
- **Reynolds number formula:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$. Critical $Re \approx 2000$.
- **Atrial kick contributes ~20–30% of ventricular end-diastolic volume.**
- **Q-S1 interval = 0.04–0.06 s** (electromechanical delay).
- **PCG frequency band:** 20–1000 Hz; sampling rate ≥ 2000 Hz (Nyquist).
- **Phonocardiography is a non-invasive, low-cost, radiation-free diagnostic modality** — a key biomedical engineering application of DSP, wavelet analysis, and AI-based classification.

> **End of Module 3 Topic: Heart Sounds & Murmurs**
> *All formulas, frequencies, timings, and clinical correlations are aligned with standard physiology references and the KTU 2024 B.Tech Biomedical Engineering syllabus for PCBMT205.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT205 - ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING (BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stroke Volume (SV) — Formal Definition

**Stroke Volume (SV)** is the volume of blood, measured in milliliters (mL), ejected by each ventricle during a single cardiac contraction (one complete heartbeat). It represents the functional output of one mechanical cycle of the heart.

Mathematically, it is the difference between the volume of blood in the ventricle at the end of diastole (filling) and the volume remaining at the end of systole (contraction/ejection).

$$\text{SV} = \text{EDV} - \text{ESV}$$

Where:
- **EDV (End-Diastolic Volume):** Volume in the ventricle just before contraction begins. Standard resting value ≈ **120 mL**.
- **ESV (End-Systolic Volume):** Volume remaining in the ventricle just after contraction ends. Standard resting value ≈ **50 mL**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Resting Stroke Volume ≈ 70 mL per beat** (range: 60–100 mL for a healthy adult at rest).

## 1.2 Cardiac Output (CO) — Formal Definition

**Cardiac Output (CO)** is the total volume of blood pumped by each ventricle per minute. It is the cornerstone measure of global cardiovascular performance and perfusion delivery to tissues.

$$\text{CO} = \text{Heart Rate (HR)} \times \text{Stroke Volume (SV)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Resting Cardiac Output ≈ 5.0 L/min** (range: 4.0–8.0 L/min in a healthy adult at rest). During peak exercise, CO can rise to **20–25 L/min** in trained athletes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Heart as a Hydraulic Pump System

Imagine the heart as a **variable-displacement water pump** connected to a closed-loop piping system (the circulatory system). Every pump stroke (heartbeat) ejects a fixed quantity of fluid (SV) into the pipe. If the pump operates at 70 strokes per minute (HR), the total water delivered per minute is simply 70 × (volume per stroke).

- **Stroke Volume** is the *size of each gulp* the pump pushes out.
- **Heart Rate** is *how often* the pump cycles per minute.
- **Cardiac Output** is the *total throughput* of the entire pumping station.

> [!TIP]
> A small, fast pump (high HR, low SV) can deliver the same throughput as a large, slow pump (low HR, high SV) — this is the principle behind the **cardiac reserve** of endurance athletes, who typically have a *higher SV at rest* and therefore a *lower resting HR*.

## 1.4 Ejection Fraction (EF) — The Efficiency Indicator

The **Ejection Fraction** is the *percentage* of end-diastolic blood that is actually ejected during each contraction. It is the single most clinically reported indicator of systolic (pumping) function.

$$\text{EF (\%)} = \frac{\text{SV}}{\text{EDV}} \times 100 = \frac{\text{EDV} - \text{ESV}}{\text{EDV}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Normal EF Range: 55% – 70%**. Values below 40% indicate **systolic heart failure**; values above 75% suggest **hyperdynamic states** (e.g., severe anemia, hyperthyroidism).

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Resting Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heart Rate | HR | 70 | beats/min |
| End-Diastolic Volume | EDV | 120 | mL |
| End-Systolic Volume | ESV | 50 | mL |
| Stroke Volume | SV | 70 | mL/beat |
| Cardiac Output | CO | 5.0 | L/min |
| Ejection Fraction | EF | 58 – 65 | % |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Determinants of Stroke Volume

Stroke volume is *not* a constant — it is dynamically regulated every single beat by three physiological variables. These are universally grouped under the **cardiac performance triad**:

### (a) Preload — The "Stretch Before the Pump"
- **Definition:** The degree of stretch of the ventricular myocardium at the end of diastole. Quantitatively approximated as **EDV** or **end-diastolic pressure (EDP)**.
- **Mechanism:** Greater venous return → more filling → more stretch of sarcomeres → stronger contraction (within physiological limits).
- **Governing Law:** **Frank–Starling Law of the Heart** — *“The energy of contraction is proportional to the initial length of the cardiac muscle fiber.”*
- **Clinical Modulators:** Blood volume, venous tone, skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, body position, atrial contraction ("atrial kick").

### (b) Afterload — The "Resistance the Pump Must Overcome"
- **Definition:** The pressure or resistance the ventricle must generate to eject blood into the arterial system.
- **Left Ventricle Afterload ≈ Aortic Pressure / Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)**
- **Right Ventricle Afterload ≈ Pulmonary Artery Pressure / Pulmonary Vascular Resistance (PVR)**
- **Effect on SV:** Increased afterload → increased ESV → **decreased SV**.

### (c) Myocardial Contractility — The "Pump Strength"
- **Definition:** The intrinsic strength of ventricular contraction at a given preload and afterload (i.e., independent of fiber length).
- **Positive Inotropes** (↑ contractility → ↑ SV): Sympathetic stimulation (norepinephrine), epinephrine, digitalis, calcium ions.
- **Negative Inotropes** (↓ contractility → ↓ SV): Parasympathetic stimulation (acetylcholine via vagus), hypoxia, acidosis, β-blockers, calcium channel blockers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for engineers:** Biomedical instrumentation (e.g., impedance cardiography, echocardiography, invasive Swan-Ganz catheters) is fundamentally designed to *quantify* these three parameters non-invasively or minimally invasively. Understanding preload, afterload, and contractility is the **physiological foundation** behind ECG, PCG, and hemodynamic monitor design.

## 2.2 Determinants of Heart Rate

1. **Autonomic Nervous System (dominant regulator)**
   - Sympathetic: ↑ HR (chronotropic +)
   - Parasympathetic (Vagus): ↓ HR (chronotropic −)
2. **Hormonal Influences**
   - Epinephrine / Norepinephrine → ↑ HR
   - Thyroid hormones → ↑ HR (chronotropic baseline)
3. **Intrinsic SA Node Firing Rate** (≈ 100 bpm without autonomic tone; resting 70 bpm is due to vagal tone).
4. **Chemical Factors**
   - Hypoxia, hypercapnia, acidosis → ↑ HR
5. **Temperature**: ↑ Core temp → ↑ HR (Q₁₀ effect).

## 2.3 Cardiac Reserve

**Cardiac Reserve = Maximum CO − Resting CO**

- Resting CO ≈ **5 L/min**
- Maximum CO (exercise) ≈ **20–25 L/min** (athletes up to 35 L/min)
- **Cardiac Reserve ≈ 4–5× resting value**

> [!TIP]
> Heart failure patients have a drastically reduced cardiac reserve, often < 2× resting. Biomedical rehab devices (e.g., pacemakers, LVADs) aim to *restore* this reserve.

## 2.4 The Fick Principle — The Gold Standard for Measuring CO

The **Fick Principle** (Adolf Fick, 1870) provides a theoretical and clinical method to compute cardiac output using oxygen consumption and arteriovenous oxygen difference.

$$\text{CO} = \frac{\text{Oxygen Consumption (VO}_2\text{)}}{\text{Arterial O}_2 \text{ Content} - \text{Venous O}_2 \text{ Content}}$$

In typical numerical form:

$$\text{CO (L/min)} = \frac{\text{VO}_2 \text{ (mL/min)}}{(\text{CaO}_2 - \text{CvO}_2) \text{ (mL/L of blood)}}$$

**Standard Resting Example:**
- VO₂ = 250 mL/min
- CaO₂ = 200 mL/L
- CvO₂ = 150 mL/L
- CO = 250 / (200 − 150) = 250 / 50 = **5.0 L/min** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The Fick method is the **reference standard** against which all other CO measurement techniques (thermodilution, dye dilution, impedance cardiography, echocardiography) are validated.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | Stroke Volume definition | mL |
| 2 | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Cardiac Output (primary equation) | L/min |
| 3 | $EF = \dfrac{SV}{EDV} \times 100$ | Ejection Fraction | % |
| 4 | $CO = \dfrac{VO_2}{CaO_2 - CvO_2}$ | Fick Principle | L/min |
| 5 | $\text{Cardiac Reserve} = CO_{max} - CO_{rest}$ | Reserve capacity | L/min |
| 6 | $CO = \dfrac{MAP - RAP}{SVR}$ | Modified from Ohm's Law (for systemic circuit) | L/min |
| 7 | $MABP = CO \times SVR + CVP$ | Mean Arterial Pressure relation | mmHg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute value bars:** All absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for modulus.

## 2.6 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

- **Pacemaker Design:** Variable-rate pacemakers modulate HR to compensate for fixed or reduced SV.
- **LVADs / Artificial Hearts:** Directly manipulate SV when native contractility fails.
- **Impedance Cardiography (ICG):** Estimates SV from thoracic electrical impedance changes synchronized to the ECG.
- **Echocardiography (Doppler):** Measures velocity-time integral of blood flow through valves × cross-sectional area → real-time SV and CO.
- **Pulse Oximeters and Fick Spirometers:** Build on the Fick principle to estimate tissue oxygen delivery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CO = HR × SV from First Principles

We begin from the definition that cardiac output is the *total volume of blood ejected per unit time*. Since the heart beats a discrete number of times per minute (HR), and each beat ejects a discrete volume (SV):

**Step 1:** Express total volume ejected in *N* beats.

$$V_{total} = N \times SV$$

where $N$ is the number of beats in the chosen time interval.

**Step 2:** Define HR as the number of beats per minute.

$$N_{per \, minute} = HR$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $N = HR$ into the volume equation and divide by time (1 minute).

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO &= \frac{V_{total}}{\text{Time (1 min)}} \\
   &= \frac{HR \times SV}{1 \text{ min}} \\
   &= HR \times SV
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Because HR is defined per minute, multiplying HR (beats/min) by SV (mL/beat) cancels the "beat" units, leaving mL/min, which is converted to L/min by dividing by 1000.

**Step 4:** Final unit conversion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO \text{ (mL/min)} &= HR \text{ (beats/min)} \times SV \text{ (mL/beat)} \\
CO \text{ (L/min)}  &= \frac{HR \times SV}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Verification** (resting adult):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO &= 70 \text{ beats/min} \times 70 \text{ mL/beat} \\
   &= 4900 \text{ mL/min} \\
   &= 4.9 \text{ L/min} \quad (\approx 5 \text{ L/min}) \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Ejection Fraction Relation

Starting from the definition of SV and EF:

**Step 1:** Write SV in terms of EDV and ESV.

$$SV = EDV - ESV$$

**Step 2:** Write the definition of EF.

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2.

$$EF = \frac{EDV - ESV}{EDV}$$

**Step 4:** Express as a percentage.

$$EF (\%) = \frac{EDV - ESV}{EDV} \times 100$$

**Step 5: Numerical Verification** (resting adult):

$$
\begin{aligned}
EF &= \frac{120 - 50}{120} \times 100 \\
   &= \frac{70}{120} \times 100 \\
   &= 58.33 \% \quad (\text{within normal 55–70\% range}) \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Fick Principle for CO

**Step 1:** Fick's assumption — the rate of oxygen uptake by the pulmonary capillaries equals the rate at which the lungs deliver oxygen to the blood.

**Step 2:** Express mass balance on oxygen.

$$VO_2 = CO \times (CaO_2 - CvO_2)$$

where $VO_2$ is the rate of oxygen consumption (mL O₂/min), $CaO_2$ is arterial oxygen content, and $CvO_2$ is venous oxygen content.

**Step 3:** Solve algebraically for CO.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO \times (CaO_2 - CvO_2) &= VO_2 \\
CO &= \frac{VO_2}{CaO_2 - CvO_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Verification.**

Given: $VO_2 = 250$ mL O₂/min, $CaO_2 = 200$ mL O₂/L blood, $CvO_2 = 150$ mL O₂/L blood.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CO &= \frac{250 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{min}}{200 - 150 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{L blood}} \\
   &= \frac{250}{50} \\
   &= 5.0 \text{ L/min} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Physiological Changes on CO

| Physiological Change | Effect on HR | Effect on SV | Effect on CO | Engineering/Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exercise (moderate) | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑↑ (×4 to ×7) | Stress ECG, treadmill test |
| Hemorrhage (mild) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ or normal | Hypovolemia monitor |
| Hypertension (↑ SVR) | ↑ (reflex) | ↓ (↑ afterload) | ↔ or ↓ | BP cuff, arterial line |
| Heart Failure (↓ contractility) | ↑ (compensation) | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | Echocardiography, BNP assay |
| Sympathetic activation | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑↑ | ICU hemodynamic monitor |
| Vagal stimulation | ↓↓ | ↔ | ↓↓ | Carotid sinus massage |
| β-blocker drug | ↓ | ↓ (mild) | ↓ | Cardiac rehab device calibration |
| Increased blood volume (transfusion) | ↑ (mild) | ↑ (↑ preload) | ↑ | Fluid management system |
| Hyperthyroidism | ↑↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | Resting tachycardia diagnosis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Determinants of Cardiac Output

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture governing cardiac output — useful for KTU diagrammatic questions and biomedical instrumentation design.

    A --> C[Total Peripheral Resistance TPR]

    B --> D[Heart Rate HR]
    B --> E[Stroke Volume SV]

    D --> F[Autonomic Nervous System]
    D --> G[Hormonal Control]
    D --> H[Intrinsic SA Node Rate]

    E --> J[Preload EDV]

    E --> L[Contractility Myocardial Strength]

    J --> J1[Venous Return]
    J --> J2[Blood Volume]
    J --> J3[Atrial Contraction]

    K --> K1[Systemic Vascular Resistance]
